food_subgroup: Tortilla chip-based snack with vegetable toppings

preparation_method: served with sauces (salsa, sour cream, or chutney)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of vegetable nachos is approximately 200 g, comprising a bed of crispy corn tortilla chips topped with fresh vegetables and sauces, typically served as a starter or snack for one to two people.

nutrient_source.title: Tortilla chips, plain (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 19056) combined with estimated vegetable and sauce topping contribution
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Indian-restaurant-style vegetable nachos exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for plain tortilla chips (NDB 19056, ~489 kcal/100 g) blended with an estimated 40% vegetable and sauce topping component (tomatoes, onions, bell peppers, salsa, sour cream) to reflect a plated restaurant dish at approximately 200 g total. The blended estimate yields approximately 320 kcal/100 g for the composite dish. Macros for the vegetable and sauce component are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for raw tomatoes (NDB 11529), raw onions (NDB 11282), raw bell peppers (NDB 11821), and commercial salsa (NDB 06164). Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Corn chips, plain — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to composite vegetable nacho dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 63 for plain corn chips (tested value range 42–74, reference glucose = 100). Vegetable toppings (tomatoes, onions, bell peppers) have negligible GI contribution. The composite dish GI is estimated at 63 (medium), consistent with the corn chip base dominating the glycemic response. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (63 × 42.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 200 g serving / 100) / 100 = 53.0 g GL. This is a high GL per serving, reflecting the large portion size of corn chips.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~840 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid vegetable nachos due to the high sodium content from seasoned corn chips and sauces, which can contribute significantly to daily sodium limits of 2000 mg recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (63) and very high glycemic load per serving (~53 GL units for a 200 g portion)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with vegetable nachos: while the GI is medium (63), the large carbohydrate load from corn chips per serving produces a high glycemic load (~53 GL), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion control and pairing with protein or fat are strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~3.8 g/100 g) and high sodium (~420 mg/100 g) from chips and dairy-based toppings
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume vegetable nachos sparingly, as the combination of saturated fat from cheese or sour cream toppings and high sodium from seasoned chips may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles; choosing reduced-fat toppings and smaller portions can mitigate risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Nachos (also known as Veg Nachos or Nachos Veg) is a popular snack dish served at Indian restaurants in Singapore, consisting of a generous bed of crispy corn tortilla chips topped with an assortment of fresh vegetables such as diced tomatoes, onions, bell peppers, jalapeños, and coriander, accompanied by savory sauces including salsa, sour cream or yogurt-based dip, and sometimes melted cheese. The dish is an Indian-restaurant adaptation of the Mexican-American nacho concept, often incorporating Indian-influenced spice profiles and chutneys alongside or in place of traditional toppings. The texture contrasts crunchy chips with soft, juicy vegetable toppings. Nutritionally, vegetable nachos are energy-dense (~320 kcal/100 g) due to the fried or baked corn chip base, with moderate fat (~14.5 g/100 g) and significant carbohydrate (~42 g/100 g). The dish is high in sodium (~420 mg/100 g) from seasoned chips and sauces, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is medium (~63) based on the corn chip base, but the glycemic load per standard 200 g restaurant serving is high (~53 GL units), making it a food that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume with caution and in controlled portions. The vegetable toppings contribute dietary fibre (~3.2 g/100 g), vitamin C (~12 mg/100 g), and vitamin A (~45 mcg RAE/100 g). The dish is vegetarian and typically halal-certified in Singapore Indian restaurants. It is commonly served as a starter or sharing snack.
common_user_queries: What are the calories in vegetable nachos?
How much sodium is in nachos?
What is the GI of nachos?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Indian-restaurant-style vegetable nachos exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy tortilla chip data blended with estimated vegetable and sauce topping contributions. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database corn chip value. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source data for this composite dish and is not expected to be a significant contributor in a corn chip and vegetable preparation. All other -1 fields have been replaced with estimated values where defensible source data was available. The food_name has been normalised from the input 'Nachos Veg' to the canonical English form 'Vegetable Nachos'; the original menu name 'Nachos Veg' is preserved in aliases. The cuisine tag 'Chicken Dish' in the input is incorrect for this vegetarian dish and has been disregarded. The dish is classified as a snack rather than a mixed dish given its primary identity as a chip-based appetizer. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (63) × carbohydrate_g per 100g (42.0) × serving size (200g) / 100 / 100 = 53.0 GL units. This is a high GL per serving. No zero-value core macros are present in this record.
